Single-cell transcriptomics of tumor-infiltrating CAR-T cells at day 14 confirmed maintained effector gene expression programs and absence of exhaustion signatures in the triple-armored group. The patent claims the multi-armored vector constructs, engineered T cells, and methods of treating desmoplastic solid tumors.
disease: pancreatic cancer; ovarian cancer; solid tumors
keywords: TME resistance; TGF-beta; dnTGFbRII; adenosine; A2AR; hyaluronidase; PH20; desmoplastic; solid tumor; multi-armored; immune suppression

title: Anti-NKG2D ligand chimeric antigen receptor natural killer cells derived from cord blood with enhanced cytotoxicity for solid tumors
text_chunk: The present invention provides chimeric antigen receptor-modified natural killer (CAR-NK) cells derived from umbilical cord blood expressing an anti-NKG2D ligand (NKG2DL) CAR construct for immunotherapy of solid tumors that overexpress stress-induced NKG2D ligands (MICA, MICB, ULBP1-6). NK cells offer several advantages over T cells as a CAR effector platform: they do not cause graft-versus-host disease when used allogeneically (enabling off-the-shelf manufacturing from unrelated cord blood donors), they possess intrinsic anti-tumor cytotoxicity through natural cytotoxicity receptors (NKp30, NKp44, NKp46) and antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) via CD16, and they produce a distinct cytokine profile (IFN-gamma, GM-CSF) with lower IL-6 and minimal IL-1 secretion, reducing the risk of severe cytokine release syndrome. The CAR construct utilizes the full-length extracellular domain of NKG2D as the ligand-binding moiety (rather than a conventional scFv), which naturally engages all eight NKG2D ligands with appropriate affinity (Kd 0.5-50 nM depending on ligand), providing broad tumor recognition while minimizing antigen escape through simultaneous targeting of multiple stress ligands. The intracellular signaling domain employs the DAP12 adaptor protein containing an ITAM motif, which is the physiological signaling partner for activating NK cell receptors, in place of CD3-zeta, enabling optimal NK cell activation. The construct additionally co-expresses membrane-bound IL-15/IL-15Ralpha fusion protein for enhanced in vivo persistence and a soluble PD-L1 trap (sPD-L1 decoy receptor) to counteract PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint-mediated suppression. Manufacturing from a single cord blood unit yields 10-50 billion CAR-NK cells following 14-21 days of ex vivo expansion on irradiated K562 feeder cells expressing membrane-bound IL-21 and 4-1BB ligand. The CAR-NK cells are cryopreserved and stored as off-the-shelf aliquots (>5 patient doses per cord blood unit). In orthotopic glioblastoma (U87MG) and ovarian cancer (SKOV3) xenograft models, NKG2D CAR-NK cells demonstrated significant tumor regression and prolonged survival compared to unmodified NK cells or conventional anti-HER2 CAR-T cells, with no signs of GvHD or neurotoxicity.
keywords: CAR-NK; natural killer cell; cord blood; NKG2D; MICA; MICB; DAP12; IL-15; PD-L1 trap; off-the-shelf; solid tumor

title: mRNA-encoded chimeric antigen receptor for transient T-cell modification with repeat dosing capability
text_chunk: This patent application describes methods for generating transiently modified chimeric antigen receptor T cells through in vitro transcribed (IVT) messenger RNA (mRNA) electroporation, providing a non-integrating CAR delivery platform that enables repeat dosing and iterative dose titration for improved safety in solid tumor applications. Unlike lentiviral or retroviral vector-mediated CAR delivery that results in permanent genomic integration and lifelong CAR expression, mRNA-encoded CARs are expressed transiently for 3-7 days following electroporation, after which CAR surface density declines to undetectable levels as the mRNA is degraded by cellular ribonucleases. This self-limiting expression kinetics provides an inherent safety advantage: any on-target off-tumor toxicity is temporally restricted and self-resolving, and the therapeutic window can be explored through iterative dose escalation with successive infusions. The IVT mRNA construct incorporates several modifications for enhanced translational efficiency and stability: 5' cap-1 structure (m7GpppA(2'-OMe)), 5' and 3' UTRs from human beta-globin, codon-optimized CAR coding sequence, and a 150-nucleotide poly(A) tail. Nucleoside modifications (N1-methylpseudouridine replacement for all uridines) reduce innate immune activation through TLR7/8 and RIG-I, preventing type I interferon-mediated translational shutdown and inflammatory cytokine production. Electroporation parameters are optimized for primary human T cells using a flow electroporation system (MaxCyte GT) enabling processing of 10^9-10^10 T cells in <30 minutes with >90% transfection efficiency, >70% viability, and CAR surface expression on >80% of viable cells at 24 hours post-electroporation. The mRNA CAR-T cells demonstrate cytotoxic activity equivalent to lentivirally transduced CAR-T cells during the expression window. A Phase I clinical trial evaluating anti-mesothelin mRNA CAR-T cells in mesothelioma and pancreatic cancer patients demonstrated safety with repeated intravenous dosing (3 infusions per cycle, 28-day cycles), transient tumor trafficking confirmed by SPECT imaging of indium-111-labeled CAR-T cells, and evidence of epitope spreading (development of endogenous anti-tumor immune responses against non-targeted antigens). The patent covers the mRNA constructs, electroporation methods, repeat dosing protocols, and treatment methods for solid malignancies.
keywords: mRNA CAR; transient expression; electroporation; non-integrating; repeat dosing; N1-methylpseudouridine; MaxCyte; dose titration; safety

title: Fully automated closed-system CAR-T cell manufacturing device with integrated process analytics and 5-day production cycle
text_chunk: The present invention provides a fully automated, closed-system benchtop device for point-of-care manufacturing of chimeric antigen receptor T cells within a 5-day production cycle, addressing critical bottlenecks in centralized CAR-T manufacturing including lengthy vein-to-vein time (currently 3-6 weeks), logistical complexity of cryopreserved leukapheresis shipment, product losses during transportation, and manufacturing costs exceeding $100,000 per batch. The device integrates all manufacturing unit operations within a single-use disposable cassette: T-cell selection (CD4/CD8 immunomagnetic positive selection from whole leukapheresis product), activation (anti-CD3/CD28 nanomatrix reagent automatically dispensed from on-cassette reservoirs), lentiviral transduction (automated vector addition at optimized MOI with spinoculation via integrated centrifuge module), ex vivo expansion (temperature-controlled gas-permeable culture chamber with automated media exchange guided by real-time glucose/lactate/pH sensors), and final formulation (automated volume reduction, wash, and cryoprotectant addition). The 5-day accelerated manufacturing protocol leverages recent findings that minimally expanded CAR-T cells with a less differentiated phenotype (Tscm/Tcm > 70%) demonstrate superior in vivo expansion and anti-tumor efficacy compared to conventionally expanded products (9-14 day culture). Integrated process analytical technology (PAT) includes impedance-based cell counting, fluorescence monitoring of T-cell activation markers (CD25, CD69), and inline flow cytometry for transduction efficiency assessment. The device operates in a cleanroom-independent manner (closed system maintains ISO Class 5 equivalent internal air quality) and requires only 2 hours of operator hands-on time across the 5-day process. Clinical-scale validation studies demonstrated consistent product quality across 50 manufacturing runs: median CD3+ purity >95%, transduction efficiency 25-45%, viability >90%, fold expansion 3-8x, total cell dose 50-200 million CAR+ cells, with 100% manufacturing success rate. The patent covers the automated device, disposable cassette design, software control algorithms, and accelerated manufacturing protocols.
keywords: automated manufacturing; closed system; point-of-care; 5-day process; disposable cassette; PAT; decentralized; Tscm; accelerated; benchtop device

title: Scalable suspension-culture AAV vector production platform for CRISPR-mediated CAR transgene knock-in manufacturing
text_chunk: This patent application discloses a scalable adeno-associated virus (AAV) vector production platform optimized for generating high-titer AAV6 donor template vectors used in CRISPR-mediated site-specific CAR transgene knock-in to the TRAC locus of human T cells. AAV6 has emerged as the preferred donor template delivery vehicle for homology-directed repair (HDR)-mediated CAR knock-in due to its high transduction efficiency in activated T cells (>80% at MOI 10^5), minimal immunogenicity, and ability to deliver donor templates up to 4.7 kb encompassing the full CAR expression cassette flanked by homology arms. However, the field faces a critical supply bottleneck: CRISPR knock-in CAR-T manufacturing requires approximately 10^5-10^6 AAV vector genomes per T cell, and clinical-scale production of 10^8-10^9 CAR-T cells therefore requires 10^13-10^15 total AAV vector genomes per patient. The disclosed platform utilizes a stable HEK293-derived producer cell line adapted to serum-free suspension culture in chemically defined media, with stably integrated AAV rep/cap genes (serotype 6) and an inducible AAV-ITR-flanked donor template under cumate-responsive promoter control. Induction with cumate triggers vector production without the variability and scalability limitations of triple-plasmid transient transfection. The suspension culture process is scaled in single-use stirred-tank bioreactors from 2L to 200L, achieving volumetric titers of 10^11-10^12 vector genomes per milliliter (a 50-100 fold improvement over adherent cell-based transient transfection methods). Downstream purification employs affinity chromatography using AAVX resin followed by anion exchange polishing and tangential flow filtration, achieving >95% capsid purity, <10% empty capsid content (assessed by analytical ultracentrifugation), and undetectable levels of host cell protein and residual DNA. A single 50L bioreactor run produces sufficient AAV6 vector for >500 patient-scale CAR-T manufacturing batches. The patent covers the stable producer cell lines, scalable production methods, purified AAV6 compositions, and their use in CRISPR-mediated CAR-T cell engineering.
keywords: AAV6; adeno-associated virus; CRISPR knock-in; HDR; TRAC locus; stable producer; suspension culture; bioreactor; scalable; vector production

title: Antibody-drug conjugate and CAR-T cell sequential combination therapy with ADC-mediated antigen upregulation for synergistic anti-tumor activity
text_chunk: This patent application describes a sequential combination therapy strategy employing antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) pre-treatment to enhance chimeric antigen receptor T-cell efficacy through ADC-mediated target antigen upregulation, immunogenic cell death induction, and tumor microenvironment conditioning. The invention is based on the discovery that sublethal doses of tubulin-targeting ADCs (specifically, anti-HER2 trastuzumab deruxtecan [T-DXd] and anti-Trop2 sacituzumab govitecan [SG]) induce a stress response in tumor cells that upregulates multiple surface antigens relevant to CAR-T targeting. Specifically, ADC treatment at IC20-IC30 concentrations increases surface expression of HER2 (2.5-fold), mesothelin (3.1-fold), EGFR (1.8-fold), and MHC class I molecules (4.2-fold) through activation of the DNA damage response pathway (ATR-CHK1-E2F1 axis) and the unfolded protein response (IRE1alpha-XBP1 branch). This antigen upregulation persists for 5-10 days following a single ADC dose, creating a therapeutic window for enhanced CAR-T cell recognition and killing. Additionally, the bystander effect of the topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd or SN-38) released from internalized ADCs induces immunogenic cell death (ICD) in tumor cells, characterized by calreticulin surface translocation, ATP release, and HMGB1 secretion, which recruits and activates dendritic cells for cross-presentation of tumor-associated antigens, potentially enabling epitope spreading. The sequential protocol involves: ADC administration on day -7 to -5, followed by lymphodepleting chemotherapy on days -5 to -3, and CAR-T infusion on day 0. In a HER2-low breast cancer PDX model (HER2 IHC 1+), T-DXd pretreatment followed by anti-HER2 CAR-T cells achieved complete regression in 80% of mice, compared to 20% with CAR-T alone and 10% with T-DXd alone. Intratumoral analysis revealed 5-fold greater CAR-T infiltration in ADC-pretreated tumors, attributed to chemokine upregulation (CXCL9, CXCL10) and reduced stromal barrier integrity. The patent claims the combination treatment methods, dosing schedules, and patient selection strategies based on ADC-induced antigen modulation biomarkers.
keywords: ADC combination; antibody-drug conjugate; T-DXd; antigen upregulation; immunogenic cell death; sequential therapy; bystander effect; HER2-low; epitope spreading

title: IL-7/CCL19-secreting armored CAR-T cells with chemokine-mediated endogenous immune cell recruitment for cold tumor conversion
text_chunk: The invention provides chimeric antigen receptor T cells engineered to constitutively secrete interleukin-7 (IL-7) and the chemokine CCL19 (C-C motif chemokine ligand 19, also known as MIP-3beta/ELC) to convert immunologically cold solid tumors into hot tumors through establishment of tertiary lymphoid structure (TLS)-like immune niches within the tumor microenvironment. Immunologically cold tumors are characterized by T-cell exclusion, low MHC class I expression, absence of type I interferon signaling, and lack of organized lymphoid structures, rendering them resistant to both checkpoint immunotherapy and conventional CAR-T therapy. IL-7 provides survival and homeostatic proliferative signals to naive and memory T cells, maintaining the endogenous T-cell compartment that is critical for sustaining anti-tumor immunity beyond the CAR-targeted antigen. CCL19 is the cognate ligand for CCR7, expressed on naive T cells, central memory T cells, and mature dendritic cells, and is a critical organizer of T-cell zone architecture in secondary lymphoid organs. Co-secretion of IL-7 and CCL19 by tumor-infiltrating CAR-T cells creates a chemokine gradient that recruits CCR7+ endogenous T cells and dendritic cells to the tumor site, while IL-7 sustains their survival and proliferative capacity. The dual cytokine/chemokine payload is encoded in a quadricistronic lentiviral vector (CAR-T2A-IL7-P2A-CCL19-IRES-EGFRt) achieving coordinate expression. In a CT26 syngeneic colon cancer model (immunocompetent BALB/c mice), IL-7/CCL19-armored CAR-T cells induced formation of organized TLS-like structures within the tumor stroma containing B-cell follicles (B220+), T-cell zones (CD3+CD4+CD8+), and mature dendritic cells (CD11c+MHC-II+), as confirmed by multiplex immunohistochemistry and spatial transcriptomics. These TLS-like structures correlated with de novo priming of endogenous tumor-specific T-cell responses against non-CAR-targeted tumor antigens (epitope spreading), leading to rejection of antigen-negative tumor variants and establishment of long-term protective immunity against tumor rechallenge. The armored CAR-T cells achieved complete regression of established cold tumors in 75% of mice, compared to 15% for conventional CAR-T cells. The patent claims the quadricistronic vector constructs, armored CAR-T cells, and methods for treating immunologically cold solid tumors.
keywords: IL-7; CCL19; armored CAR-T; tertiary lymphoid structures; cold tumor; hot tumor; chemokine; dendritic cell recruitment; epitope spreading; CCR7

title: In vivo CAR-T cell generation using lipid nanoparticle-delivered mRNA with T-cell-targeted ionizable lipid formulation
text_chunk: This patent application discloses a transformative approach to chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy through direct in vivo generation of CAR-T cells by intravenous administration of T-cell-targeted lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) encapsulating mRNA encoding the CAR construct, eliminating the need for ex vivo T-cell manufacturing entirely. The technology addresses the fundamental scalability, cost, and access limitations of current autologous CAR-T manufacturing by converting CAR-T generation from a complex cell manufacturing process into a simple intravenous injection. The T-cell-targeting LNP formulation incorporates a novel ionizable lipid (ALC-0315 derivative with branched alkyl tails optimized for endosomal escape in T lymphocytes), DSPC (distearoylphosphatidylcholine), cholesterol, and a PEG-lipid conjugate decorated with anti-CD5 or anti-CD3 F(ab')2 antibody fragments covalently linked via maleimide-PEG-DSPE for selective T-cell uptake. The anti-CD5 targeting moiety provides selective T-cell transfection (>70% of circulating CD3+ T cells at 24 hours post-injection) with minimal uptake by non-T-cell populations (<5% of monocytes, <2% of B cells), as demonstrated by flow cytometric analysis of peripheral blood mononuclear cells. The mRNA cargo incorporates the same nucleoside modifications (N1-methylpseudouridine) and structural elements validated in COVID-19 mRNA vaccines, achieving peak CAR surface expression at 24-48 hours post-injection with transient expression lasting 5-10 days per dose. In a disseminated Raji lymphoma model, a single intravenous dose of anti-CD19 CAR-mRNA-LNPs (0.5 mg/kg mRNA) achieved >60% tumor regression, with efficacy enhanced to >90% complete regression with three weekly doses. Repeat dosing was well-tolerated with no evidence of anti-PEG antibody-mediated accelerated blood clearance through four consecutive weekly doses. The transient nature of mRNA expression provides inherent safety (self-limiting CAR expression), while repeat dosing capability enables dose optimization and sustained anti-tumor pressure. Estimated manufacturing cost is <$10,000 per treatment course compared to >$400,000 for autologous CAR-T cell products. The patent covers the T-cell-targeted LNP formulations, mRNA constructs, dosing regimens, and methods for in vivo CAR-T cell generation.
keywords: in vivo CAR-T; lipid nanoparticle; LNP; mRNA; T-cell targeting; anti-CD5; ionizable lipid; injectable; off-the-shelf; cost reduction
journal: Patent: Capstan Therapeutics / University of Pennsylvania
